name: Castro Urdiales and Northern Route Loop
name_original: Santa María de la Asunción y Castillo-Faro de Santa Ana en Castro Urdiales – circular desde Las Arenillas
description: This difficult 20.9-mile touring cycling route from Las Arenillas offers historical landmarks and panoramic coastal views in Castro Urdiales
region_intro: Touring cycling around Castro-Urdiales offers diverse landscapes, from the Cantabrian Sea coastline to green mountains inland. The region features high cliffs, coves, and beaches, contrasting with lush mountainous terrain. Greenways, often following old railway tracks, provide additional routes for exploration. This area combines coastal vistas with challenging mountain ascents and historical points of interest. Best touring cycling routes around Castro-Urdiales The most popular touring cycling route is Castro Urdiales and Northern Route Loop, a 20.9 miles (33.6 km) trail that takes 2 hours 50 minutes to complete, offering panoramic coastal views. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Valverde Tunnel – Piquillo Greenway loop from Castro-Urdiales, a difficult 72.3 miles (116.3 km) path. This route features challenging climbs into the mountains. Local touring cyclists also love the Castro Urdiales – Santa Ana Castle and Lighthouse loop from Otañes, a 29.1 miles (46.9 km) trail leading through historical landmarks and coastal views, often completed in about 3 hours 7 minutes. Touring cycling around Castro-Urdiales is defined by coastal paths, green mountains, and historical sites. The network offers options for various ability levels, from moderate coastal rides to difficult mountain ascents. The routes in Castro-Urdiales are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 100 reviews. More than 1,400 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Castro-Urdiales's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes around Castro-Urdiales?
A: Touring cycling around Castro-Urdiales offers a diverse mix of landscapes. You'll find stunning coastal paths with panoramic ocean views, lush green mountains providing challenging climbs, and tranquil greenways, often following old railway tracks. Routes can vary from mostly paved surfaces suitable for all skill levels to more rugged sections that may require advanced riding skills.

Q: Are there challenging touring cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, Castro-Urdiales provides ample opportunities for experienced touring cyclists seeking a challenge. Many routes feature significant elevation gains and demanding climbs into the mountains. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38192847" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valverde Tunnel – Piquillo Greenway loop from Castro-Urdiales&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 72.3-mile path with substantial elevation changes. The region also features climbs like Monte Cueto, known for its demanding ascents.

Q: Are there any easier touring cycling routes suitable for beginners?
A: Absolutely. While many routes offer challenges, there are also easier options perfect for beginners or those looking for a more relaxed ride. These often include gentle coastal paths and greenways. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31887871" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Punta Cotolino – Castro Urdiales loop from Castro-Urdiales&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 15.6-mile route that offers a pleasant experience without extreme difficulty.

Q: What historical landmarks or natural features can I see while cycling in Castro-Urdiales?
A: Cycling routes in Castro-Urdiales often pass by significant historical sites and natural wonders. You can explore the iconic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31928063" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santa Ana Castle and Lighthouse&lt;/a&gt; and the impressive Church of Santa María de la Asunción. The region also boasts a rich mining heritage, with some routes following old mine trails, allowing you to explore former mines. For natural beauty, you might encounter the spectacular beech woods of Cerredo or the valuable natural landscape of Candina, known for nesting griffon vultures on sea cliffs.

Q: Are there any scenic viewpoints along the touring cycling routes?
A: Yes, Castro-Urdiales is renowned for its breathtaking scenic viewpoints. Many coastal routes offer panoramic ocean vistas, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1012257450/castro-urdiales-and-northern-route-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro Urdiales and Northern Route Loop&lt;/a&gt;. Inland, ascents into the mountains, like those around Monte Cueto, reward cyclists with expansive views of the region's green landscapes and the Cantabrian Sea.

Q: When is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Castro-Urdiales?
A: The most popular times for touring cycling in Castro-Urdiales are generally July and August, when the weather is typically warm and sunny, perfect for enjoying both the coastal and mountain routes. However, the mild climate of Cantabria means that spring and early autumn also offer excellent conditions with pleasant temperatures and fewer crowds.

Q: Are there family-friendly touring cycling routes in Castro-Urdiales?
A: For families, the region offers several accessible options, particularly along its greenways and gentler coastal paths. These routes often provide a safer and more relaxed environment away from heavy traffic. While specific family-designated routes aren't listed, look for routes described as 'easy' or 'moderate' with lower elevation gains, which are generally more suitable for all ages.

Q: Can I bring my dog on touring cycling routes around Castro-Urdiales?
A: While Castro-Urdiales is a great outdoor destination, specific regulations for dogs on cycling routes can vary. Generally, dogs are welcome on many natural trails and greenways, especially if kept on a leash. However, it's always advisable to check local signage or specific route descriptions for any restrictions, particularly in protected natural areas or on private land. Ensure your dog is fit enough for the distance and terrain.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy the most about touring cycling in Castro-Urdiales?
A: The touring cycling routes in Castro-Urdiales are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 100 reviews. Cyclists frequently praise the stunning contrast between the spectacular coastline and the lush green mountains, the variety of terrain, and the opportunity to combine physical activity with exploring historical landmarks and natural features.

Q: Are there any lakes or mountain peaks I can visit near the cycling routes?
A: Yes, the area around Castro-Urdiales features several beautiful lakes and impressive mountain peaks that can be explored. You can find highlights like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4096819" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pozo Hostión&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2939178" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lakes of the Old Iron Mines&lt;/a&gt;. For mountain enthusiasts, peaks such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4486690" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Solpico Summit&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2839887" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ganeran Summit&lt;/a&gt; offer challenging climbs and rewarding views.

Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Castro-Urdiales?
A: There are over 65 touring cycling routes available around Castro-Urdiales. These routes cater to various skill levels, with 5 easy, 19 moderate, and 41 difficult options, ensuring there's a suitable ride for almost every cyclist.
tips: Arenillas beach is a small beach of fine sand next to the Oriñón estuary. The beach disappears at high tide, but at low tide, in addition to the beach itself, there is also a strip of sand that joins it with Oriñón beach. In addition, the beach, at low tide, is a popular area with surfers, although you have to be careful with the underwater currents. In fact, on the left side of the beach you will see an area where the buoys restrict access due to currents.

The Church of Santa María de la Asunción in the town and municipality of Castro-Urdiales (Spain), locally known as the Cathedral of Santa María (although not a cathedral), is a Gothic-style Catholic temple built between the 13th and 12th centuries. and XV. Century. It lies in front of the Cantabrian Sea, next to the lighthouse castle and next to the city's fishing port.

The Castle dates from the year 1163 and is located next to the Church of the Assumption. It was first used as a lighthouse in 1853. You can visit it inside (morning: 11 a.m. to 1:30 p.m. and afternoon: 6 p.m. to 8 p.m.). Outside it is full of seagulls and from there you can see impressive views, you can even see the Basque Country from afar. A must if you visit this small city in Cantabria!

Castro Urdiales is a Cantabrian fishing town, next to Euskadi, between sea and hills. It is full of history and among its monuments, the church of Santa María and the castle of Santa Ana stand out. Its beaches attract bathers and its promenade invites you to take a walk while enjoying a spectacular view.

A 400-meter tunnel that connects Mioño with Castro Urdiales, crossing the slopes of the Cueto hill. It is used by both pedestrians and cyclists. From Castro Urdiales to Mioño there is a slight climb, but without problems. Around there is a thick vegetation where ferns and mosses are good indicators of the humidity that surrounds this natural environment.

The church of San Julián is from the beginning of the s. XVI. Several reforms have been carried out, although it keeps its old Gothic layout. Inside we can see in the apse a sculpture by Juan de Garay from the s. XVII. The former presence of an image of Santiago links this church to the Jacobean route.
https://iglesiasdecantabria.blogspot.com/2009/05/iglesia-de-santullan.html

faqs.question: What kind of fitness level is required for this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: This route is categorized as a "difficult" touring bicycle ride, requiring a very good fitness level. It features significant elevation gain and demanding climbs, making it best suited for experienced cyclists.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: The estimated completion time for this circular touring bicycle route is around 2 hours and 50 minutes, covering approximately 33.6 kilometers (20.8 miles).

faqs.question: What historical landmarks and scenic views can I expect along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter a rich blend of natural beauty and historical sites. Key highlights include the impressive Church of Saint Mary of the Assumption (Castro Urdiales), the historic Santa Ana Castle and Lighthouse offering panoramic coastal views, and the charming town of Castro Urdiales itself. You'll also pass by Arenillas Beach and the Valverde Tunnel.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: The route is described as having mostly paved surfaces. However, with a substantial elevation gain of nearly 790 meters, you should expect steep sections and challenging climbs, typical of a hard bike ride.

faqs.question: Is there parking available near the starting point in Las Arenillas?
faqs.answer: Yes, you should be able to find parking options near Arenillas Beach, which is the starting point for this circular route. It's a semi-urban area, so street parking or small lots may be available.

faqs.question: Is this touring bicycle route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While there are no specific restrictions against dogs on this route, it's important to consider that it's a challenging touring bicycle ride with significant paved sections and potential traffic in urban areas. If you bring your dog, ensure they are comfortable with long distances, can keep pace, and are always on a leash, especially near roads and populated areas.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The coastal climate of Cantabria generally offers pleasant cycling conditions from spring through early autumn (April to October). During these months, you'll likely experience milder temperatures and less rain, making the demanding climbs more enjoyable and the coastal views clearer. Always check the local weather forecast before you go.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or is there an entrance fee for this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this public touring bicycle route. However, if you plan to visit the interior of landmarks like the Santa Ana Castle and Lighthouse or the Church of Saint Mary of the Assumption, there might be separate entrance fees for those attractions.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with any other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this touring bicycle route incorporates sections of several other paths. Notably, it covers a significant portion of the Northern Route (Cantabria) (44%) and the Senda del Litoral (33%). You'll also find smaller segments of paths like the Pista de los Alemanes and the Antiguo ferrocarril Castro Urdiales – Alén.

faqs.question: Are there places to stop for food or drinks along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through Castro Urdiales, you'll find numerous cafes, restaurants, and shops where you can refuel and refresh. The town offers plenty of options for a break, especially near the port and historical center.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a challenging touring bicycle ride like this?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and length, it's essential to bring plenty of water, energy snacks, a repair kit for your bicycle, and appropriate cycling gear. Sun protection (sunscreen, sunglasses) is recommended, especially along the coast. A helmet is always advised for safety.

waypoints.name: Church of Saint Mary of the Assumption (Castro Urdiales)
Church of San Julián de Santullán
